# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I need a good psychological thriller to watch ASAP

**Score:** 59
**Body:** Can someone please recommend a psychological thriller that for me? Something that is a mind f\*ck and has an amazing story behind it.

My favorite movies:

\- pulp fiction

\- prisoners

\- law abiding citizen

\- shawshank

\- the dark knight

\- no country for old men

\- memento \*\*

a bit basic, I know but I’m not the biggest movie buff and I am trying to get into it.

I’m happy to accept YouTube movies as recs too! I know there are 100% a ton of hidden gems on there.

Thanks

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ex Machina 
2. The Game
3. Silence of the Lambs 
4. Strange Darling. don't read any spoilers 
  - This movie is hardly ever mentioned in these movie subs. It’s so good!
5. The Gift (2015)
6. Invisible Man 
7. Identity (2003) with John Cussack. 
8. Jacob's Ladder
9. Coherence is a low budget mind bender that punches way above its weight.

## 2. Most Disturbing Real Life Documentaries that are Non-Crime related

**Score:** 50
**Body:** Hello can anyone suggest a deeply disturbing or depraved documentaries that does not involve murder or crime but rather something that delves into real lives of different kinds of people? It can be psychological, addiction, or anything as long as it does not deal with murder or crime. Thank youu

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Grizzly Man
2. Three Identical Strangers. Probably a spoiler, because it looks heartwarming from ads.
3. Love Has Won: The Cult of Mother God
4. Jesus Camp (2006)
5. There is a YouTube channel called Soft White Underbelly that you might find interesting. They are interviews with disenfranchised people, rather than documentaries, but so interesting. 

For documentaries, you can never go wrong with Grey Gardens. 
  - Grey Gardens is the best!
6. Blackfish
7. Tickled
8. Seriously there’s March of the Penguins that will leave you gutted. The whole egg debacle
9. The Way Down, about a weight loss/“Christian” cult. The leader is a trip.

## 3. Anything similar to Princess Bride?

**Score:** 43
**Body:** I'm looking for similar light-hearted movies with witty dialogue and a lot of replay value. I've already seen Stand By Me and all Monty Python movies, so I need something else to scratch that itch. Anything post-1970s will work for me. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Stardust
2. Galaxy Quest. We rewatch as often as Princess Bride and it's equally quotable 
3. For a flawless and light-hearted movie of recommend “Back to the Future” and its sequels. 
4. The Goonies

Jumanji
5. Labrynth
6. Willow
7. "Eric the Viking" Top drawer cast, funny as hell. I think I'm the only one who loves it.
8. The Court Jester, Ladyhawke, A Knights Tale
9. Clue 
10. Robin Hood: Men in Tights

## 4. Movies where actual members of the subculture make a movie based on the subculture?

**Score:** 27
**Body:** I’m watching SLC punk and thoroughly enjoying it, but it’s made by people who are not punks and staring people who are not punks and it got me thinking, is there a movie where punks made it and it punks played the characters. 

This can be any culture like gothic, emo, e-boy/girl or hip hop ect.. 

Documentaries are also fine but I’m more so wanting movies with this idea. 

A movie like mid90s but with actual people within the skate culture, or trainspotting with addicts. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The movie Kids(1995) is dark, disturbing, and about authentic as it gets. It's scripted, but close to a documentary. But it has just about every trigger warning you can think of. I watched it once and that was it. There's a reason actors play roles in movies. 
2. ***Krush Groove*** **(1985)** is about the early days of Def Jam Records, and stars actual Musicians of the day, playing themselves - no pun intended.
  - Oh that sounds awesome 
3. This not the most apropos assignment, but, for me, it’s the most interesting one.

Head (1968)

It was an attempt by The Monkees to tear away from their sculpted “pre-fab four” image and reclaim what legitimate generational cred they actually did have.

Produced by Jack Nicholson a full year before breaking waves with Dennis Hopper and Easy Rider, the film actually has some success in gathering counterculture aesthetics and ideas and arriving before the larger cultural explosion.
4. Most of Kevin Smith's early movies are about the slacker subculture he was part of.
  - Which was inspired by Richard Linklater’s movie “Slacker”, about the slacker subculture he was a part of.
5. City of God - Rio’s underculture as told by someone who grew up in it and photographed it for national geographic
6. Suburbia (1983) I've suggested this movie twice in one day, what are the odds? Anyway, Penelope Spheeris directed it after she made the documentary "The Decline of Western Civilization" about the punk scene in the 80s. When the documentary initially wasn't gaining traction, she made Suburbia and she hired a bunch of street kids and punks to star in it (also Flea from RHCP) and several punk bands played in it as well. Great movie.
7. Here are a few biker movies made by bikers

The Proud Rider (1971) made by the Satan’s Choice Oshawa Chapter. 

Hells Angels Forever (1980) documentary on the Hell’s Angels by the Hell’s Angels

Dead in 5 Heartbeats (2013) a film by Sonny Barger production on a fictional motorcycle club but this was made by bikers with real MC members throughout.  

I’ll say that the first two are available on YouTube the last is available on Tubi. If you’re looking for Oscar worthy acting, these recommendations won’t be for you. But they fit the category. 
8. Any Alex Cox movie

## 5. What is a really American movie?

**Score:** 23
**Body:** So it's the fourth of July in the US. 

I realize not everyone here is American, but I'm having this conversation with someone...

What is the most "American" Movie of all time?

My vote is Rocky. Not only for the actual story behind the film but also the incredible achievement that became the film.

Obviously there are countless options, but what's your opinion?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sandlot 
2. Jaws is the epitome of a Fourth of July movie 
3. American Graffiti 
4. Forrest Gump. Take a walk through American history with Forrest Gump as your guide.
5. Team America: World Police
  - Nothing says ’enjoy your Independence Day‘ like puppet sex
6. The Right Stuff (1983)
7. Little big man.
8. Independence Day 
9. Jackie Brown

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which movie character instantly makes your blood boil? I just finished watching Saving Private Ryan for the first time after a friend recommended it, and I have never wanted to scream at a screen more than I did at Corporal Upham.

**Score:** 1344

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Percy from the green mile
  - The actor is a crook bloke IRL too
2. Mrs. Carmody from Stephen King's The Mist. She's absolutely unbearable.

https://preview.redd.it/um7cdbcjt5bh1.jpeg?width=267&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=880e8e101e0b017c9c5aa2c4d5f06313774aa07a
  - I instantly knew this is many peoples Mother and was horrified by her acting. What a great job. 
3. I used to really hate this character but the older I get the more I realize he was just a normal person put into a horrible situation. People process traumatic events differently, and honestly I have no idea how I would react in the situation like he was in.
  - And Upham really had no business being there in the first place. Like I get he was the only option available to Captain Miller in a priority mission directly from the Chief of Staff, but still he was a cartographer at a desk job sent in with a squad of battle hardened Rangers that not only had never seen combat but rightfully never expected to given what his job was. 
  - >honestly I have no idea how I would react in the situation like he was in.

Yah, genuinely... I think people who hate/can't stand a character like this live slightly in a fantasy land. They dont like the character because they believe *they* would never act like that, when the reality is most of us a priviledged enough to never have to find out. They think they would he heroic, stoic, a tough guy.

But the majority of us would be exactly like him.

That realisation for some comes with time and maturity.
4. I thought his character captured the fear of war really well. Of course as a viewer, it's disturbing and frustrating but people react differently in these situations. Gave the human perspective. Not everyone is a hero.
5. Umbridge
6. Upham is so incredibly misunderstood. He was a typist from the rear echelon who got thrown into the thick of it with these battle hardened veterans. He wasn’t a warrior, he was an office assistant in his early twenties who hadn’t fired a rifle since basic training. He had no business being in battle. When he was on the stairwell he was literally so terrified that he was paralyzed with fear. It’s an incredibly human response to that horrific scenario and not a single person who rants about him could say that they’d do any better. Story-wise he serves an important purpose of humanizing the action on the screen and being an avatar for the viewer, lest this movie become a cliche John Wayne flick where everyone is always a badass and nobody succumbs to human nature or falls victim to the indescribable fear unleashed by war. People also bitch about him later taking the German squad prisoner and shooting the guy who killed the Captain. They’re totally misreading that scene as well. Upham wasn’t suddenly pretending to be a hero or a badass. The nightmare he just lived through finally broke him, and he shot the guy at that breaking point. That’s what war does to young men. It doesn’t create heroes, it breaks boys, physically and mentally.

We aren’t necessarily meant to \*like\* Upham or what he does, but we also aren’t meant to vilify him for it either. We’re meant to see, through him, the abject horror of war, how dehumanizing and demoralizing it really is, and how it breaks the spirit and will of the people stuck in it. The person he was going in isn’t the person he is coming out, and because of that Upham is, despite living through the battle, also a casualty of war.

## 2. What’s your favorite movie with Justin Long?

**Score:** 584

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He started with a bang: Galaxy Quest 
2. Probably Barbarian and he was a total jack wagon in it but he did a great job at it. Loved that movie.
3. Jeepers creepers, or Accepted
  - Love Accepted and how often they played it on Comedy Central
4. His bit in Zack & Miri is amazimg
  - All-male casts? Like "Glengarry Glen Ross"?

….Like "Glen and Gary suck Ross's meaty cock and drop their hairy nuts in his eager mouth."
    - “Is that a sequel?”
      - More of a reimagining. 
5. Dodgeball: A True Underdog Story. Also, barbarian
6. Accepted 

## 3. What is your favorite Christina Ricci movie?

**Score:** 554
**Body:** She was amazing in Buffalo 66 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Adams Family Values
  - Her dead pan line delivery in both movies was fantastic.

“Are they made from real Girl Scouts?”

Or when the other girl is like “I’ll play the victim!” then Wednesday just dead pans “all your life” and the creepy as shit smile she forces at camp.
2. Casper 
3. Black Snake Moan
4. Sleepy Hollow / Adam's Family Values 
5. https://preview.redd.it/4om4js3ls4bh1.jpeg?width=2048&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=ec81aff722f6de5d164728be2349391efbe59b15

  
monster is very special to me
6. https://i.redd.it/6m5sohqxs4bh1.gif
7. Speed racer. She is fantastic and looks even better 
8. ![gif](giphy|gmQNYr9nnbXxu)
9. https://preview.redd.it/zwqpinaqo4bh1.jpeg?width=1000&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=cc3e8ce9dc495a6ffbcdfce46c723bf29e8dd1f7

## 4. Bloodsport (1988) As a kid, this was peak cinema.

**Score:** 541

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My divorced dad rented this from Blockbuster every week for a year I swear 
  - My dad rented Spy Game on three separate occasions without realising he'd already rented it. He rented it two weekends in a row and then about a month later rented it again. We kept insisting we'd already seen it and he refused to accept it until about half an hour in and then went "oh yeah, we have seen this". Ridiculous.
2. It took a whole Dragonball Z episode to throw that kick
3. As an adult, this is peak cinema. “You break my record. Now I break you like I break your friend.”
  - You got the Dim Mak?
4. ![gif](giphy|KyI9Z4O7LmrenU2nN2)


5. The FINEST cinema. Not even Over the Top over the tops this. 
  - ![gif](giphy|3oxRmpE9qph8XroPFS)
6. ![gif](giphy|42wzBRi1H1CIU)  
Ok, USA!
7. Just love the text at the end "Frank Dux after winning the Kumite went on to hold a record of 600-0-0 WLD. None of these fights were ever witnessed by anybody let alone a recognised governing body. Fuck you guy!" 

## 5. I surrounded myself with yes men: the movie

**Score:** 484
**Body:** I was really intrigued to watch this because I liked the Bonnie and Clyde take from the trailers and I’m a fan of Frankenstein in general but holy shit was I taken for a rollercoaster ride and not a good one. 

It is clear from the finished product, and various interviews, that no one told Maggie Gyllenhaal no or even asked “why?” 

I did actually really like Jesse Buckley’s performance as Mary Shelly but don’t think it fits for Mary Shelly and wish she was playing a different character in a different movie 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I really enjoyed Christian Bale as Frank but I think that this movie was made to “say something” but didn’t really end up saying anything. 
  - It certainly didn’t offer a fresh take on whatever the filmmakers thought it was saying.
2. “It insists upon itself”
3. This was such a schizophrenic mess of a movie, but I couldn't stop watching it...it was enthralling to me to see where the film would veer wildly off to next.
4. I really looked forward to this film, but the first five minutes of voiceover explaining what was I to see was really disappointing. Couldn’t get past that. Show don’t tell. 
  - I couldn't get past that opening monologue. Completely turned me off to the movie.
    - It gave me edgy late 90s indie movie vibes. 

  
Actually thinking about it a bit more. Maybe late 90s edgy perfume advert vibes. 
      - Somewhere between love and hate lies… \*OBSESSION\* 
  - I felt EXACTLY the same. I was really looking forward to it too because I love Jessie Buckley and I really enjoyed The Lost Daughter so I was excited to see Maggie Gyllenhaal direct again, but OOF. 

It felt very much like the movie was following the dumb trend of having to cater to second screen viewing by overexplaining and needless exposition and repitition. I didn't get past the first 8 minutes, so I don't know if the rest of the movie is like that, but I was immediately turned off. I'll try again someday so I have a fully formed opinion of it.

It really sucks because women directors for sure get much more severe criticism and are cut less slack when they make something disappointing, bad, or even just mediocre. 
5. This shit sucked lol

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Lighthouse and Persona

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Lighthouse has been a favorite for awhile but finally recently saw Persona and was blown away. Not overhyped at all, aged amazingly. I see why Eggers praises Bergman so much but it almost changes the way I see Lighthouse.

It's definitely its own thing in it being the male psyche side of things and the Greek mythology. But even the foghorn in both is so similar it's hard not to feel like Lighthouse is a bit derivative. 

I still love it but I suppose it's more a testament to how good Persona is 60 years later. The lighting is insane, the abstraction, the acting, the taboo subject matter. But I'm sure this has been beat to death.

To be clear I still feel The Lighthouse and The Master are two modern masterpieces that contend with the classic 60s/70s films I love. Might be too early to tell but I believe they are.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Master, The Lighthouse, Mulholland Dr, 3 Women. The list of films influenced by Persona is endless. 

It is one of the most quintessential ‘art house’ films but none of it feels like a cliche. 

It feels like an impossible achievement but it also feels so simple. A great formalist vision that says all of cinema can be boiled down to two faces facing each other, even if one of them isn’t speaking. 
  - Mullholland I havent seen, I have a mental block on Lynch films I'm trying to get past. Can't get past halfway on his films. That influence is clear but it's funny I didn't even realize until you said how obviously The Master is clearly influenced by Persona
2. Persona is one of those films where once you watch it you just see its influence in everything. Very very good film (even though I cannot claim to fully understand it)

Totally see what you mean by The Lighthouse
  - I don't fully understand it either. My gut reaction was the nurse is a projection of the actress. But yeah I mean fight club first got me into film as art when I was a kid - and that's persona. The master and lighthouse is persona. You're right - its influence is everywhere
  - I haven’t seen it in such a long time. I wrote a paper about it in college. I wonder where it is? But yes, time for a rewatch. 
3. I saw Persona for the first time last summer and it completely changed how i saw many modern movies. I was also surprised how little talk there is online comparing The Lighthouse to Persona, the parallels are so obvious to me, from the script to the visuals
4. The Bergman I've seen has all been so much more watchable than one might expect of such highbrow stuff, not homework at all.

I'll have to give The Lighthouse a second chance, I wasn't crazy about it in the theater but I'm pretty sure I missed like half the dialogue. 
  - I saw it on a plane the first time, just killing time. I was shocked how good it was especially considering my viewing context

## 2. Exit 8 : Great concept, unfortunately ruined by modern Japanese movie clichés

**Score:** 17
**Body:** The story follows a simple office worker who, after getting off a train, finds himself trapped inside a long corridor. Every time the protagonist walks to the end of the corridor and turns the corner, he is shocked to discover that he has returned to the exact same starting point again, as if the place is imprisoning him inside an endless loop.

As a concept, it's excellent on paper. It immediately hooks you because you want to know the origin of this place, what it represents, and if you understand its symbolism, you'll really appreciate the film and what it's trying to do. But the problem is that it's a Japanese film. What do I mean by that? Have you ever watched anime where everything is shown clearly to the audience, and naturally you're waiting for the protagonist to react like a normal person, but instead the main character keeps acting clueless as if nothing is happening? If that's not clear enough, let me give you an example. Imagine there's a monster standing in front of you, and every time you turn your back on it, it moves. Any normal person would stop turning their back on it in an attempt to survive, because that's simply the logical thing to do. Not here. The protagonist keeps turning his back on it over and over again, and every time he turns around he makes a shocked face, then turns his back again only to discover that the monster has moved.

And this isn't just one or two scenes. The entire screenplay is written this way, as if it treats the audience like idiots, while the people inside the film are even dumber. As a result, you can expect the acting and dialogue to follow the same pattern. Since neither the dialogue nor the acting is particularly good, don't expect the characters to be engaging. Yet by the end, the film still wants you to sympathize with them and care about them.

But honestly, I can't lie and say the storytelling didn't change my opinion of the film after the first hour. Yes, it still delivers those same stupid clichés, but it introduces new characters, shows us this place from their perspectives, starts providing answers, develops the main character further, explains the foundation of the idea and why this place exists, and ultimately helps you understand the symbolism of the location and what it truly means.

On the other hand, the directing, cinematography, and color palette were consistently very good from beginning to end.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Watched this on a plane recently and thought it was unbearable. The protagonist is so clueless that it's ridiculous. The writers inexplicably gave him severe asthma so he's hacking up a lung throughout the film. The symbolism was beating you over the head. I would strongly recommend skipping this one.
2. The film is an adaptation of a video game. As someone who’s also familiar with that game, I can’t say objectively if knowledge of the game is essential to understanding the film, but what I can say is that the film is extremely faithful to the game’s premise. (So much so that I’d advise new players to avoid the movie because it gives away many puzzles.)

How the game works is like this: you’re dropped into the underpass without any context or background. You wander about aimlessly and endlessly, occasionally running into jumpscares, until at a certain point you begin to notice that the exit sign at a certain corner sometimes increments by one.

It’s only at that point you finally understand the cryptic instruction next to the sign: when you notice something odd (there’s no explanation for what’s considered odd), you must turn back; if you don’t spot anything you must proceed and not turn back. You must not make any mistakes as you walk down the underpass, (and each time a weird salaryman will pass you, as if you were in some sort of Groundhog Day) until you somehow leave the underpass.

The source of gameplay anxiety comes from the constant need for concentration as you look out for tiny anomalies in the environment. You’re constantly questioning yourself if what you’re seeing is the same as the last corridor, and every little anomaly you spot (like advertising posters that slowly grow in height, or braille tiles that hide a human face if you look closer) gives you goosebumps. What we see the film’s characters were doing replicates exactly the creeping anxiety you would experience if you were to play the game blind.

What the film adds to the barebones game is to imply the underpass is a sort of moral trial, and what the characters see while trapped is a reflection of their guilt and anxieties. Personally that’s where I think is the cliche is (it’s a trope that’s common to horror survival games), but it serves its narrative function for the film.
  - Knowing how the game works in no way saves this film from being kinda trash and I agree with OP to a large extent. Knowing the backstory and lore and depth of an existing IP to further appreciate a spin-off within the same world is one thing, but the game offers none of that. It didn't sound like you were arguing for much tbh, so I'm not trying to call you out, but you legitimately just explained the game mechanics. There's no story there. And game mechanics mean largely nothing to the movie format.

The movie was extremely boring, repetitive...not scary at all....and elicited a reaction where you wanted to yell at the characters because they did something obviously wrong.
  - As I said, the concept is very promising, and the movie delivers on that promise while expanding it in a way that makes you appreciate it even more. The idea of questioning whether the things you see every day are actually normal or anomalies is fantastic. The term anomaly refers to something that deviates from what is standard, normal, or expected. In both the movie and the game, this idea is emphasized through posters, doors, lights, and people. But metaphorically, the movie takes it much further.

It begins with the protagonist witnessing an anomaly on the train: an adult screaming at a woman carrying her child. Everyone around him ignores what they just saw and goes back to looking at their phones as if nothing happened. It's just like the loop itself. You notice an anomaly, and you're given a choice: either move forward into the corridor, ignoring the anomaly and resetting all the progress you've made, or turn back, acknowledge the anomaly, and move on to the next level. The protagonist saw the anomaly of a man screaming at a woman carrying her child, but he chose to move forward instead of confronting it. By ignoring it, he ended up trapped in Level 0. Such a great concept.

Another concept the movie introduces is what the corridor itself represents. Some interpret it as an experiment, while others see it as limbo. Either way, it's a test. The movie reinforces this idea by introducing the protagonist as "The Lost Man." Before even introducing his name, we're shown the word "Test," reinforcing what is actually happening here. You might ask, "A test for what?" That's what the whole movie is about. It's a test for people who cannot decide who they want to be or whether they're ready for the future.

In the protagonist's case, he's being tested on whether he can become a father. The movie brilliantly places a lost child, who is also trapped in the corridor, in his care, and the corridor watches to see what he'll do. If he fails, like the bald man, who failed every chance he was given by refusing to listen to the child when he found an anomaly, losing control in front of him, and even hitting him instead of becoming a good father, he'll remain trapped in that endless loop, his own version of hell. But if he proves that he can carry the responsibility, listen to his child, and protect him, as we see in the movie, then the corridor finally gives him an exit. At that point, he is no longer "The Lost Man." He has finally chosen who he wants to be. And the corridor gives him another chance to return to that moment on the train, where the man is screaming at the woman. This time, he's given a second chance to confront the anomaly instead of ignoring it.
  - Fully knowing and have played the game I’m so curious as to how they made it into a movie. I was so sure they would switch it up and only keep the title to bring in fans of the game but now that you said they’ve followed the game premise I’m so intrigued to see how it translates to the screen 
    - I watched the film thinking the game was based on it (I previously installed but forgot about the game), but it turned out to be the reverse—the film was based on the game.

I finally got around to completing the game and it explained a feeling I had throughout the film: that the characters’ behaviour was a little too “game logic-like”.

tl;dr it’s a good adaptation of the game into a film, but only a so-so expansion of the game’s premise (it’s not very scary and doesn’t quite fit the horror genre.)
3. A video game is an fluid, interactive premise. A movie is an unchanging, linear story. It's VERY hard to make one into the other. I liked a lot of the wtf moments in Exit 8 but I concur that as a whole, it doesn't hold together.

## 3. Great making-of docs

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I'm looking for making-of documentaries that work well as standalone films and are not just curios for fans of the original movie. 

The only examples I can really think of are Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse(Eleanor Coppola, 1991) and Burden of Dreams (Les Blank, 1982). 

Preferably, auteur based ones that have strong personal style rather than just being adequately made featurettes.



### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think a safe bet would be Pedro Costa's Where does your hidden smile lie? on Straub and Huillet editing Sicilia! or maybe it's just Straub in the movie though, can't remember now.. Godard called it one of the best documentaries he had seen

Costa is very much influenced by Straub & huillet - they share a similar conception of how cinema should be politically committed, even though Costa's movies are way more lyrical and lush
  - The best film on cinema ever made.
  - Saw this and Sicilia! with Pedro in attendance. Love that one.
2. Lost in La Mancha is a world class making of doc. It’s about Terry Gillingham’s frankly cursed attempts to make his Don Quixote adaptation. 

It’s film making as the impossible dream, everything that can go wrong does, it’s a mad tilt against reason.
3. Kind of obvious ones but the Werner Herzog ones:


**Burden of Dreams** about the highly tedious making of Fitzcarraldo and **My dearest Fiend** about his bizarre relationship with Klaus Kinski.



Both very good imho.
4. The Sweatbox, it’s a doc about the long making of process for The Emperors New Groove.  It’s directed by Trudie Styler, who is married to Sting who was supposed to write a Phil Collins-esque score for ENG and him being told the film is moving in a different non-musical direction is in the film.  Crazy stuff.

Heavy focus on studio filmmaking and decision making dysfunction.
5. Lost Soul: The Doomed Journey of Richard Stanley's Island of Dr. Moreau

Pretty fascinating look at one of the most cursed movies ever made. Features a lot of good anecdotes about Brando being an asshole that could easily fill a good documentary on their own, but the bizarre behaviour of Richard Stanley really elevates it.

## 4. Why Cross of Iron has more in common with Platoon than All Quiet on the Western Front

**Score:** 3
**Body:** In my opinion, Sam Peckinpah's "Cross of Iron" actually shares more similarities with Oliver Stone's "Platoon" than All Quiet on the Western Front, to an extent at least. While All Quiet on the Western Front and Cross of Iron are alike in the fact that they are told from the German perspective and have characters that believe the war is useless (even a hospital scene), they don't have much in common structurally. All Quiet focuses on the **young** Paul Baumer, while Cross focuses on the **old** Rolf Steiner (AND HE CAN ACTUALLY PULL A GIRL IN THE HOSPITAL, OH YEAH!).

Although Platoon is set at least 24 years later in the jungles of Vietnam instead of the freezing fronts of Russia, the actions done by the characters echo those of Cross of Iron. We have the civil war between Sergeant Elias and Sergeant Barnes. Barnes even shoots Elias to prevent a trial for his cruel killing of the innocent village chief's wife! We see the same civil war in Cross of Iron, in which Stransky tells Triebig to shoot Steiner and his squadron, and "disguise" it as mistaking them for the Russians. Anything to prevent Steiner from exposing him for lying about leading his so-called counter-attack earlier.

Phew. That was a mouthful, but I hope anyone reading liked it! Did I miss out on some similarities or differences? (I haven't seen Platoon in 508 days, Cross of Iron in 223) Am I reaching?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Assuming we're talking about the 1930 All Quiet on the Western Front here;

I think Platoon and All Quiet are the two movies that have the most in common. They're both about young men going to war and slowly being disillusioned by the experience. In both movies the younger man is taken under the wing of a more experienced NCO, who's eventual death completes the process of disillusionment. 

The differences between the two are that Platoon has a lot of pointed criticisms towards US policy in and around the Vietnam war while All Quiet is more of a general criticism that could be applied to any country in any war even though it's built around Germany in WW1. Furthermore, All Quiet's criticisms are largely targeted at civilians and people at the home front while the soldiers are portrayed as the victims, Platoon meanwhile villianises members of the eponymous Platoon. 

With regards to Cross of Iron, I feel the movie was built as a criticism of the war movie genre and how audiences interact with such media rather than a criticism of war itself. 
  - I like the 1930 and 2022 All Quiets, but the 1979 one is peak to me. Had fun watching it for the first time with me Dad.

## 5. Question about JFK

**Score:** 3
**Body:** Watching this and wondering why Jim Garrison of New Orleans would be prosecuting the perp for Kennedy’s assassination? It seems like that would have been in Dallas. Was it because that was the US District court that covered Texas, or was it some other reason.

Other thoughts on the Film invited. Surprised Garrison was not killed as words so many witnesses and people involved in the investigation of Kennedy’s death.

Also, is there a similar theory about Robert Kennedy being killed by the establishment or was his assassin Sirhan acting alone?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The film is a hallucination of history. I think it’s a fun one. Jim Garrison wasn’t satisfied with the Warren commission report so he began investigating what he thought was a grand conspiracy. The one person he arrested and prosecuted was acquitted in less than an hour. 

Nobody really needed to kill him. You’ve got to need to kill someone, and then want to kill them, and then actually do it. Those are three pretty big hurdles.
2. Jim Garrison basically manufactured a new Orleans case as pretext to take on the Kennedy Assassination and prosecuted an entirely innocent man Clay Shaw.  Clay Shaw literally became a suspect because he was a homosexual named Clay.  The main witness against Clay Shaw never mentioned him or an assassination planning party he attended until he was drugged with sodium pentothal and hypnotized. Stone knew he couldn't put this guy in the movie, so he invents a fictional character, the prostitute played by Kevin Bacon.

The movie is great and rousing cinema but it is 100% horseshit propaganda and nonsense as history.  It's a very good example of how even if we think we are sophisticated thinkers a sophisticated propagandist will hoodwink us every time.  I remember how I felt after watching that movie.  And how pissed off I felt when I found out it lied to me.

This book will make your jaw drop about the real Jim Garrison 

https://www.abebooks.com/9780871318794/False-Witness-Real-Story-Jim-0871318792/plp

>Was it because that was the US District court that covered Texas, or was it some other reason.

No. Jim Garrison was a state official. He was the the DA for New Orleans.  The connection to New Orleans was Oswald lived in New Orleans.  The character played by kindly old Jack lemmon was actually a pretty mean and vicious drunk and ex-con. He had a grudge against David Ferrie, the guy played by Joe Pesci.  When he learned  that Lee Harvey Oswald had once been in the Civil Air Patrol in Louisiana he started calling TV stations and newspapers and cops saying David Ferrie was part of the conspiracy because he was a squad leader in the Civil Air Patrol.  This was investigated back in '64 and Ferrie was completely cleared, because once this guy sobered up three days later, he stopped making these claims.  

>Surprised Garrison was not killed as words so many witnesses and people involved in the investigation of Kennedy’s death.

You've been hoodwinked.

Nobody was killed because of the Kennedy investigation.  People died.  Because that's what happens when time moves forward.  The idea there was all these connected deaths to the assassination was started by a paranoid newspaper editor in Texas, Penn Jones. He lived until 1998.  The other guy making these claims was Mark Lane.  he was much more famous And died at age 89 in 2016.  When they actually looked at how tenuous some of these deaths were connected to the Kennedy assassination, they realized you would be dealing with a pool of tens of thousands of people.  And once you start dealing with tens of thousands of people, you're going to have unusual deaths.  Insurance companies actually look at this sort of thing and figure out what is the likelihood of a pool of people dying and they found out Kennedy witnesses were actually far safer than the general population. They died at a far fewer rate
3. Well, first off, the film is fiction. Second, Jim Garrison was a lunatic, so no one was interested in going after him. Third, yes, there are conspiracy theories about RFK and they’re just as BS as the JFK theories.
